instruction: Demonstrate GDB and LLDB advanced debugging patterns for C++: pretty-printing STL containers, conditional hardware watchpoints, reverse debugging, and analyzing core dumps from production crashes.
output:
<think>
GDB and LLDB are the primary debuggers for C++ on Linux and macOS respectively. Let me cover the advanced features that are essential for production debugging.

Advanced GDB features:
1. Pretty-printing: display STL containers readably (std::vector, std::map, etc.)
2. Hardware watchpoints: break when a specific memory address changes (no overhead)
3. Conditional breakpoints with complex expressions
4. Reverse debugging (record-and-replay with rr or GDB's built-in)
5. Core dump analysis: post-mortem debugging without running the process
6. Python scripting for custom commands

LLDB equivalents:
- Pretty-printing: built-in formatters for libc++ containers
- Watchpoints: `watchpoint set variable x`
- Conditional: `breakpoint modify -c 'condition'`
- Core dumps: `lldb -c core.dump executable`

I'll write C++ code that demonstrates scenarios you'd debug with these tools, with inline comments showing the debugger commands.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <map>
#include <string>
#include <memory>
#include <thread>
#include <mutex>
#include <signal.h>
#include <cstring>

// ===== Data structures for GDB pretty-printing =====

struct Employee {
    std::string name;
    int id;
    double salary;
    std::vector<std::string> skills;
};

class Department {
public:
    std::string name;
    std::map<int, std::shared_ptr<Employee>> employees;
    
    void addEmployee(std::shared_ptr<Employee> emp) {
        employees[emp->id] = emp;
    }
    
    // GDB: p dept.employees
    // Shows: std::map with 3 elements = {[1] = shared_ptr<Employee> (use count 1)...}
    // GDB: p *dept.employees[1]
    // Shows: {name = "Alice", id = 1, salary = 95000, skills = vector of length 2...}
};

// ===== Memory corruption target for watchpoints =====

struct Config {
    int magic = 0xDEADBEEF;
    int max_connections = 100;
    int timeout_ms = 5000;
    char hostname[64] = "localhost";
};

void corruptConfig(Config& cfg) {
    // GDB: watch cfg.magic
    //   Hardware watchpoint: breaks when magic changes
    // GDB: watch -l cfg.max_connections
    //   Watch specific memory location
    
    // Bug: buffer overflow corrupts adjacent fields
    // strcpy(cfg.hostname, very_long_string); // would overflow into magic
    cfg.max_connections = 999; // legitimate change, watchpoint fires
}

// ===== Multithreaded code for thread-aware debugging =====

std::mutex g_mutex;
int g_counter = 0;

void worker(int id, int iterations) {
    // GDB: break worker if id == 3
    //   Only breaks for worker thread 3
    // GDB: thread apply all bt
    //   Print backtrace of ALL threads
    // LLDB: thread list
    //   Show all threads with their current function
    
    for (int i = 0; i < iterations; ++i) {
        std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(g_mutex);
        g_counter++;
        
        // GDB: break here if g_counter > 50
        //   Conditional on global state
    }
}

// ===== Signal handler for core dump generation =====

void setupCoreDump() {
    // Enable core dumps
    // Shell: ulimit -c unlimited
    // GDB: gdb ./program core.12345
    //   Opens the core dump for post-mortem analysis
    // GDB commands in core dump:
    //   bt         - backtrace at crash point
    //   info threads - all threads at crash
    //   thread apply all bt - all thread backtraces
    //   p variable - inspect variable values
    //   info locals - all local variables
    //   info registers - CPU register state
}

// ===== Crash-inducing code for core dump analysis =====

void deepFunction(int depth, int* ptr) {
    // GDB on core: bt shows the full call stack to the crash
    if (depth == 0) {
        // Null pointer dereference - generates SIGSEGV
        // In production, this creates a core dump
        // *ptr = 42; // uncomment to crash
        std::cout << "  Reached depth 0 safely (crash line commented out)\n";
        return;
    }
    int local_data[4] = {depth, depth*2, depth*3, depth*4};
    // GDB on core: info locals shows local_data values
    deepFunction(depth - 1, ptr);
}

void crashScenario() {
    int* bad_ptr = nullptr;
    // GDB: p bad_ptr  -> 0x0
    deepFunction(5, bad_ptr);
}

int main() {
    std::cout << "=== GDB/LLDB Debugging Demo ===\n\n";
    
    // STL container debugging
    Department eng;
    eng.name = "Engineering";
    
    auto alice = std::make_shared<Employee>(
        Employee{"Alice", 1, 95000.0, {"C++", "Rust"}});
    auto bob = std::make_shared<Employee>(
        Employee{"Bob", 2, 88000.0, {"Python", "Go", "JS"}});
    auto carol = std::make_shared<Employee>(
        Employee{"Carol", 3, 102000.0, {"C++", "Systems"}});
    
    eng.addEmployee(alice);
    eng.addEmployee(bob);
    eng.addEmployee(carol);
    
    // GDB: p eng
    // GDB: p eng.employees
    // GDB: p *eng.employees[1]
    // GDB: p alice.use_count()
    std::cout << "Department: " << eng.name << " (" 
              << eng.employees.size() << " employees)\n";
    
    // Watchpoint target
    Config cfg;
    corruptConfig(cfg);
    std::cout << "Config max_connections: " << cfg.max_connections << "\n";
    
    // Multithreaded debugging
    std::cout << "\nStarting workers...\n";
    std::vector<std::thread> threads;
    for (int i = 0; i < 4; ++i) {
        threads.emplace_back(worker, i, 25);
    }
    for (auto& t : threads) t.join();
    std::cout << "Counter: " << g_counter << "\n";
    
    // Core dump scenario
    std::cout << "\nCrash scenario (safe mode)...\n";
    crashScenario();
    
    // Reference card
    std::cout << "\n=== GDB Quick Reference ===\n";
    std::cout << "Start:     gdb ./program\n";
    std::cout << "Core:      gdb ./program core.dump\n";
    std::cout << "Attach:    gdb -p <pid>\n";
    std::cout << "\n";
    std::cout << "Breakpoints:\n";
    std::cout << "  b main.cpp:42          (line)\n";
    std::cout << "  b worker if id==3      (conditional)\n";
    std::cout << "  watch cfg.magic        (hardware watchpoint)\n";
    std::cout << "  rwatch cfg.magic       (read watchpoint)\n";
    std::cout << "\n";
    std::cout << "STL pretty-printing:\n";
    std::cout << "  p my_vector            (shows elements)\n";
    std::cout << "  p my_map               (shows key:value)\n";
    std::cout << "  p my_shared_ptr        (shows pointer + use_count)\n";
    std::cout << "\n";
    std::cout << "Threads:\n";
    std::cout << "  info threads           (list all)\n";
    std::cout << "  thread 3               (switch to thread 3)\n";
    std::cout << "  thread apply all bt    (all backtraces)\n";
    std::cout << "\n";
    std::cout << "Reverse debugging (with rr):\n";
    std::cout << "  rr record ./program    (record execution)\n";
    std::cout << "  rr replay              (replay in GDB)\n";
    std::cout << "  reverse-continue       (run backwards)\n";
    std::cout << "  reverse-step           (step backwards)\n";
    
    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement Valgrind and AddressSanitizer usage patterns for detecting memory errors in C++: buffer overflows, use-after-free, memory leaks, and uninitialized reads, with practical examples and interpretation of error reports.
output:
<think>
Valgrind (Memcheck) and AddressSanitizer (ASan) are the two primary tools for detecting memory errors in C++. They work differently:

Valgrind: Dynamic binary instrumentation — runs the program in a virtual CPU. No recompilation needed. 20-50x slowdown.
ASan: Compile-time instrumentation — adds checks at compile time. Needs -fsanitize=address. 2x slowdown.

What they detect:
- Buffer overflow (heap, stack, global)
- Use-after-free
- Use-after-return (ASan with detect_stack_use_after_return)
- Memory leaks
- Uninitialized memory reads (Valgrind only, or MSan)
- Double-free
- Invalid free

I'll write C++ code with intentional bugs (commented out to prevent actual crashes), show what the tools would report, and demonstrate the fixed versions.

For each bug:
1. The buggy code
2. The tool output (as comments)
3. The fix

I'll also show UBSan (UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer) for integer overflow, null dereference, etc.
</think>

```cpp
#include <cstdlib>
#include <cstring>
#include <iostream>
#include <memory>
#include <vector>

// Compile for debugging:
//   g++ -g -O0 -fsanitize=address,undefined -o debug_demo debug_demo.cpp
// Or use Valgrind:
//   g++ -g -O0 -o debug_demo debug_demo.cpp
//   valgrind --leak-check=full --track-origins=yes ./debug_demo

// ===== Bug 1: Heap buffer overflow =====

void heapBufferOverflow() {
    std::cout << "--- Heap Buffer Overflow ---\n";
    int* arr = new int[10];
    for (int i = 0; i < 10; ++i) arr[i] = i;
    
    // BUG: writing past the end (commented out for safety)
    // arr[10] = 99;
    // ASan output:
    //   ERROR: AddressSanitizer: heap-buffer-overflow on address 0x...
    //   WRITE of size 4 at 0x... thread T0
    //   #0 heapBufferOverflow() debug_demo.cpp:20
    //   allocated by thread T0 here:
    //   #0 operator new[](unsigned long)
    //   #1 heapBufferOverflow() debug_demo.cpp:18
    
    // FIX: bounds checking
    std::vector<int> safe_arr(10);
    // safe_arr.at(10) = 99; // throws std::out_of_range
    
    delete[] arr;
    std::cout << "  (overflow line commented out)\n";
}

// ===== Bug 2: Use-after-free =====

void useAfterFree() {
    std::cout << "--- Use After Free ---\n";
    int* ptr = new int(42);
    delete ptr;
    
    // BUG: accessing freed memory
    // int val = *ptr;
    // ASan output:
    //   ERROR: AddressSanitizer: heap-use-after-free on address 0x...
    //   READ of size 4 at 0x...
    //   freed by thread T0 here:
    //   #0 operator delete(void*)
    //   previously allocated by thread T0 here:
    //   #0 operator new(unsigned long)
    
    // FIX: use smart pointers
    auto safe_ptr = std::make_unique<int>(42);
    // After reset, safe_ptr is nullptr — clean crash, not silent corruption
    safe_ptr.reset();
    // *safe_ptr would be a null dereference, not use-after-free
    
    std::cout << "  (use-after-free line commented out)\n";
}

// ===== Bug 3: Memory leak =====

void memoryLeak() {
    std::cout << "--- Memory Leak ---\n";
    
    // BUG: allocated but never freed
    // int* leaked = new int[1000];
    // Valgrind output:
    //   ==12345== 4,000 bytes in 1 blocks are definitely lost
    //   ==12345==    at operator new[](unsigned long)
    //   ==12345==    by memoryLeak() (debug_demo.cpp:55)
    // ASan with -fsanitize=address (includes LeakSanitizer):
    //   ERROR: LeakSanitizer: detected memory leaks
    //   Direct leak of 4000 byte(s) in 1 object(s) allocated from:
    //   #0 operator new[](unsigned long)
    
    // FIX: RAII
    auto safe = std::make_unique<int[]>(1000);
    // Automatically freed when safe goes out of scope
    
    std::cout << "  (leak line commented out)\n";
}

// ===== Bug 4: Stack buffer overflow =====

void stackBufferOverflow() {
    std::cout << "--- Stack Buffer Overflow ---\n";
    char buffer[16];
    
    // BUG: writing past stack buffer
    // strcpy(buffer, "this string is definitely too long for the buffer");
    // ASan output:
    //   ERROR: AddressSanitizer: stack-buffer-overflow on address 0x...
    //   WRITE of size 50 at 0x...
    
    // FIX: use bounded copy
    strncpy(buffer, "short", sizeof(buffer) - 1);
    buffer[sizeof(buffer) - 1] = '\0';
    
    // Better FIX: use std::string
    std::string safe_buffer = "any length is fine";
    
    std::cout << "  buffer: " << buffer << "\n";
}

// ===== Bug 5: Uninitialized memory read =====

void uninitializedRead() {
    std::cout << "--- Uninitialized Read ---\n";
    int values[5];
    // values[0] through values[4] are uninitialized
    
    // BUG: using uninitialized value in condition
    // if (values[2] > 0) { ... }
    // Valgrind output:
    //   ==12345== Conditional jump depends on uninitialised value(s)
    //   ==12345==    at uninitializedRead() (debug_demo.cpp:87)
    //   ==12345==  Uninitialised value was created by a stack allocation
    //   ==12345==    at uninitializedRead() (debug_demo.cpp:83)
    
    // FIX: initialize
    int safe_values[5] = {0};
    if (safe_values[2] > 0) {
        std::cout << "  positive\n";
    }
    
    std::cout << "  (uninit read line commented out)\n";
}

// ===== Bug 6: Double free =====

void doubleFree() {
    std::cout << "--- Double Free ---\n";
    // int* ptr = new int(42);
    // delete ptr;
    // delete ptr; // DOUBLE FREE
    // ASan output:
    //   ERROR: AddressSanitizer: attempting double-free on 0x...
    
    // FIX: unique_ptr prevents double-free by design
    auto safe = std::make_unique<int>(42);
    // Cannot accidentally free twice
    
    std::cout << "  (double-free lines commented out)\n";
}

// ===== UBSan: Undefined Behavior Sanitizer =====

void undefinedBehavior() {
    std::cout << "--- Undefined Behavior ---\n";
    
    // Signed integer overflow (UB in C++)
    // int x = INT_MAX;
    // int y = x + 1; // UB!
    // UBSan output:
    //   runtime error: signed integer overflow: 2147483647 + 1
    //   cannot be represented in type 'int'
    
    // Null dereference
    // int* p = nullptr;
    // int v = *p;
    // UBSan output:
    //   runtime error: load of null pointer of type 'int'
    
    // Shift overflow
    // int shifted = 1 << 33; // UB for 32-bit int
    // UBSan: shift exponent 33 is >= the width of type 'int'
    
    std::cout << "  (UB lines commented out)\n";
}

int main() {
    std::cout << "=== Memory Debugging Demo ===\n";
    std::cout << "Compile: g++ -g -fsanitize=address,undefined -o demo demo.cpp\n";
    std::cout << "Valgrind: valgrind --leak-check=full ./demo\n\n";
    
    heapBufferOverflow();
    useAfterFree();
    memoryLeak();
    stackBufferOverflow();
    uninitializedRead();
    doubleFree();
    undefinedBehavior();
    
    std::cout << "\n=== Tool Comparison ===\n";
    std::cout << "ASan:     2x slowdown, compile-time, catches most errors\n";
    std::cout << "Valgrind: 20-50x slowdown, no recompile, catches uninit reads\n";
    std::cout << "MSan:     Catches uninit reads (like Valgrind but faster)\n";
    std::cout << "UBSan:    Catches undefined behavior (overflow, shifts, null)\n";
    std::cout << "TSan:     Catches data races (like Go's -race)\n";
    std::cout << "\n";
    std::cout << "Recommended CI flags:\n";
    std::cout << "  -fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g\n";
    
    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement techniques for analyzing core dumps from production C++ crashes: extracting stack traces, examining thread states, inspecting smart pointer contents, and reconstructing the crash context.
output:
<think>
Core dump analysis is essential for post-mortem debugging of production crashes. The core file contains the entire memory state of the process at the time of the crash.

Key techniques:
1. Generating core dumps: ulimit, systemd-coredump, /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern
2. Opening in GDB: gdb executable core.dump
3. Stack trace analysis: bt, bt full, thread apply all bt
4. Thread state inspection: info threads, thread N
5. Variable inspection: p, x, info locals
6. Smart pointer inspection: p *my_shared_ptr, p my_unique_ptr.get()
7. Memory inspection: x/20x address (hex dump)
8. Signal analysis: info signal, why did it crash?

I'll write a C++ program that demonstrates scenarios you'd encounter in core dump analysis, with extensive comments showing GDB commands and their output.

For production:
- Always compile with -g (debug symbols) even in release builds
- Ship symbols separately (split-debuginfo)
- Use core_pattern to send cores to a crash reporting service
- Use minidumps (Google Breakpad/Crashpad) for smaller dumps
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <map>
#include <string>
#include <memory>
#include <thread>
#include <mutex>
#include <csignal>
#include <cstdlib>

// Compile with debug symbols for core dump analysis:
//   g++ -g -O1 -o service service.cpp -pthread
//
// Enable core dumps:
//   ulimit -c unlimited
//   echo '/tmp/core.%e.%p' | sudo tee /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern
//
// Analyze:
//   gdb ./service /tmp/core.service.12345

// ===== Application structures =====

struct Request {
    int id;
    std::string method;
    std::string path;
    std::map<std::string, std::string> headers;
    std::vector<uint8_t> body;
};

struct Connection {
    int fd;
    std::string remote_addr;
    std::shared_ptr<Request> current_request;
    bool is_active;
};

class RequestHandler {
public:
    virtual ~RequestHandler() = default;
    virtual std::string handle(const Request& req) = 0;
};

class ApiHandler : public RequestHandler {
    std::map<std::string, std::string> cache_;
public:
    std::string handle(const Request& req) override {
        // GDB on core:
        //   p req.path
        //   p req.headers
        //   p this->cache_
        //   p this->cache_.size()
        return "OK: " + req.path;
    }
};

class Server {
    std::vector<std::shared_ptr<Connection>> connections_;
    std::unique_ptr<RequestHandler> handler_;
    std::mutex mutex_;
    bool running_ = true;

public:
    Server() : handler_(std::make_unique<ApiHandler>()) {}

    void addConnection(std::shared_ptr<Connection> conn) {
        std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);
        connections_.push_back(conn);
    }

    // GDB on core:
    //   p this->connections_.size()
    //   p *this->connections_[0]
    //   p this->connections_[0]->current_request
    //   p *this->connections_[0]->current_request
    //   p this->handler_  (shows unique_ptr contents)
    void processConnection(int index) {
        std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);
        
        if (index >= static_cast<int>(connections_.size())) {
            std::cerr << "Invalid index: " << index << std::endl;
            return;
        }
        
        auto& conn = connections_[index];
        if (!conn || !conn->current_request) {
            std::cerr << "Null connection or request" << std::endl;
            return;
        }
        
        auto response = handler_->handle(*conn->current_request);
        std::cout << "Response: " << response << std::endl;
    }

    void dumpState() const {
        // This function helps reconstruct crash context
        // GDB: call server.dumpState() — can call functions on core!
        std::cout << "\n=== Server State ===\n";
        std::cout << "Connections: " << connections_.size() << "\n";
        std::cout << "Running: " << running_ << "\n";
        for (size_t i = 0; i < connections_.size(); ++i) {
            auto& c = connections_[i];
            std::cout << "  [" << i << "] fd=" << c->fd
                      << " addr=" << c->remote_addr
                      << " active=" << c->is_active;
            if (c->current_request) {
                std::cout << " req=" << c->current_request->method
                          << " " << c->current_request->path;
            }
            std::cout << "\n";
        }
    }
};

// ===== Core dump analysis helper =====

void printCoreAnalysisGuide() {
    std::cout << R"(
=== Core Dump Analysis Guide ===

1. OPEN CORE DUMP:
   gdb ./service /tmp/core.service.12345

2. CRASH LOCATION:
   bt                     # backtrace
   bt full                # with local variables
   frame 3                # select stack frame
   info locals            # variables in current frame
   info args              # function arguments

3. ALL THREADS:
   info threads           # list all threads
   thread 2               # switch to thread 2
   thread apply all bt    # ALL thread backtraces
   thread apply all bt 5  # top 5 frames per thread

4. CRASH SIGNAL:
   info signal            # what signal caused the crash
   # SIGSEGV = null/bad pointer, SIGABRT = assert/abort,
   # SIGFPE = divide by zero, SIGBUS = alignment error

5. SMART POINTERS:
   p my_shared_ptr                    # shows pointer value
   p *my_shared_ptr                   # dereference
   p my_shared_ptr._M_ptr             # internal pointer (libstdc++)
   p my_shared_ptr.use_count()        # reference count
   p my_unique_ptr._M_t._M_t._M_head_impl  # internal (libstdc++)  

6. STL CONTAINERS:
   p my_vector                # pretty-printed contents
   p my_vector.size()         # element count
   p my_map                   # key-value pairs
   p my_string                # string content

7. MEMORY INSPECTION:
   x/20x 0x7fff...           # hex dump 20 words
   x/s 0x7fff...             # as string
   info proc mappings        # memory map

8. REGISTER STATE:
   info registers            # all registers
   p $rip                    # instruction pointer
   p $rsp                    # stack pointer
   x/5i $rip                 # disassemble at crash point

9. PRODUCTION TIPS:
   - Always compile with -g (even release builds)
   - Use split-debuginfo to keep symbols separate
   - Consider Google Breakpad for structured crash reports
   - Log recent operations for context reconstruction
)";
}

int main() {
    Server server;
    
    // Set up some state for core dump inspection
    auto req1 = std::make_shared<Request>(Request{
        1, "GET", "/api/users",
        {{"Content-Type", "application/json"}, {"Authorization", "Bearer xyz"}},
        {}
    });
    
    auto conn1 = std::make_shared<Connection>(Connection{
        42, "192.168.1.100:54321", req1, true
    });
    
    auto conn2 = std::make_shared<Connection>(Connection{
        43, "10.0.0.1:12345", nullptr, false
    });
    
    server.addConnection(conn1);
    server.addConnection(conn2);
    
    server.processConnection(0);
    server.dumpState();
    
    printCoreAnalysisGuide();
    
    return 0;
}
```
